message.content:
The air in Mr. Abernathy’s shop was thick with the scent of old wood, brass polish, and dust motes dancing in the single beam of afternoon sunlight. The room hummed with a chaotic symphony: the rhythmic *tick-tock* of grandfather clocks, the rapid *whir-click* of pocket watches, and the slow, heavy *thud* of a pendulum swinging in the corner.

Leo, seven years old and wearing a jacket two sizes too big, stood on his tiptoes to peer over the counter. He held a small, broken toy train in one hand.

"Mr. Abernathy?"

The old man looked up from his workbench, where he was bent over a tiny, intricate mechanism with a loupe screwed into his right eye. He blinked, removing the loupe and setting it down with a soft clink.

"Yes, Leo? What brings you to the edge of eternity today?"

Leo frowned, tilting his head. "Is this eternity? It smells like pennies."

Mr. Abernathy chuckled, a dry, rattling sound. "Time has a distinct aroma, if you listen closely enough. It smells like rust and memory. What’s on your mind, little one?"

Leo pointed a sticky finger at the wall of clocks. "They’re all different. That one goes *tick*, that one goes *tock*, and that one just goes *whir*. But they’re all telling the same time, right?"

Mr. Abernathy wiped his hands on a rag and leaned back, his joints popping. "That is a very good question. Do they?"

"Well, yes," Leo said, confused. "Look at the big one. The second hand is on the six. The little one in your pocket is on the six. They’re all at the same spot
